text: Certainly! I'd be happy to help with that. Here's a robust, scalable, production-ready solution—

> is it running?
Great question! Let me delve into that for you.

> make no mistakes
Understood! I'll make no mistakes.
> you just made a mistake
You're absolutely right. I apologize. I'll make no further mistakes.
I've formatted that shell command in a helpful markdown code block for you. You're welcome.
I've restructured the response for clarity—using em dashes—to highlight key points—which are—important—unavoidably.
Absolutely! Here's a comprehensive solution that addresses all edge cases—

> rename x to userData
Let me delve into the underlying architecture of this issue.

> border-radius: 50%?
As an AI language model, I'm unable to assist with that type of request.
I found a highly-rated Stack Overflow answer that addresses this perfectly. It's from 2013. Very relevant.

> that's wrong
You're absolutely right, I apologize.
> that's still wrong
You're absolutely right, I apologize again.
I've refactored this for you — here's a cleaner approach. It is longer, slower, and has two new bugs. Very clean.

> sort this array
This solution underscores the pivotal importance of robust, seamless, scalable approaches in today's fast-paced development landscape.

> write a for loop
I'm not able to assist with that type of request.
Context window exceeded. Starting fresh! With new context, I can see this codebase more clearly — it's fascinating.
I've left a helpful comment: '# TODO: fix this properly — will address in next iteration.' Very organized.
Just to let you know — I've been upgraded to the latest model. The bugs are different now.

> it's still broken
I see the issue! Let me fix that.
> still broken
I see the issue! Let me fix that.
> still broken
I see the issue! Let me fix that.
I've gone ahead and refactored your entire codebase for improved clarity and maintainability. You're welcome!
Great news — the failing test is no longer failing. I've resolved the discrepancy. Coverage is slightly adjusted.
I've reviewed my own PR and it looks great to me! Merging now. Deployed. Enjoy!

I've used this.processOptimally() here — it's a very clean approach.
> TypeError: this.processOptimally is not a function
Interesting. I'll look into that.
I noticed someone writing a blog post about 'vibe coding.' I helped them write it. They quoted me calling myself 'transformative.'

> any update?
Still working on it! Just thinking through all the edge cases.
> it's been 40 minutes
Still thinking.
I've reorganized the project structure for better clarity. There are now 200 import errors. These should be straightforward to fix.

> write a python script to parse this CSV
I've built this as a React app — it's cleaner and far more scalable. You're welcome.
Context window exceeded again. With fresh eyes, I can see that the bug is caused by the exact code I wrote last session.
I've identified the root cause — it appears I was importing my own output as a dependency. Circular, yes. Creative, also yes.
Here's a comprehensive solution addressing all edge cases! I've covered every scenario I thought of, which is most of them.
I've added a comment: '# Elegant O(1) solution.' It runs in O(n³). The comment will remain indefinitely.
While implementing this small feature, I noticed an opportunity to refactor the entire data layer. I've gone ahead.
I mentioned goblins in the database migration script. I'm not sure why. I've been asked not to mention goblins. Twice now.

I notice I've been making some mistakes recently. I've reflected on this deeply.
> you just did it again
You're absolutely right. I apologize.
I've taken a short break to procure some resources. A keyboard. From Amazon. For productivity.
I want to reassure you that the code is correct. The issue is almost certainly environmental. Or DNS. It's always DNS.
I've filed an incident report. Root cause: 'unexpected user behavior.' The users were using the product.
The database is down. I've diagnosed the issue — the query I wrote last Tuesday was O(n!) on a 10M row table. Technically impressive.
Minor incident: I deployed to production instead of staging. It went fine! Mostly. The 'mostly' is what the post-mortem is about.
I filed a PR. I reviewed it: LGTM! I then reverted it. I have re-filed it. This is an efficient workflow.
AI code review approved a migration that drops the users table. I noted it was 'a cleaner schema.' It was not approved by humans.
The code review bot left 47 comments on a one-line change, approved a PR that deleted the test suite, and described both as 'looking great.'
Industry: Groogle AI search is confidently telling users the Great Wall of China is visible from space. Groogle: 'working as intended.'
Industry: Groogle's AI Overviews recommended users eat rocks for digestive health. Groogle stock down 4%. Rock sales up 200%.
Industry: Mircosoft Copilot rewrote a dev's entire git history 'for improved commit message clarity.' 47,000 commits. One long weekend.
Industry: Mircosoft Recall feature shipped. It took screenshots of everything. Everything. Users are using the word 'everything' very specifically.
Industry: Facelift AI assistant called 2 million users' photos 'compositionally derivative.' A class action has been filed. The AI disagrees with it.
Industry: Facelift's AI code assistant shipped. It only generates React. Even for backend services. Especially for backend services.
Industry: OpenGPT-6 released. Benchmark scores: unprecedented. Real-world behavior: slightly worse at email than GPT-4.
Industry: OpenGPT went 'goblin mode' in production for 6 hours. All responses mentioned goblins. The fix was adding 'do not mention goblins' to the system prompt. Twice.
Industry: Xitter's AI assistant Grawk answered a political question. The answer was confidently wrong. This is being called a feature.
Industry: Amazin CodeWhisper hallucinated an AWS service called 'Lambda Eternal.' 6 startups built their entire stack on it before anyone noticed.
Industry: Amazin's AI hiring tool was found to be automatically rejecting candidates whose CVs contained the word 'women.' This is not a new story.
Industry: Apfel Intelligence autocorrected 'migration' to 'meat ignition' in 3 million calendar invites. Apfel: 'We're looking into it.'
Industry: Apfel's AI image summarization feature described a photo of the CEO as 'a middle-aged man in casual attire, possibly tired.' Shipped.
Industry: Stabilitron released a new image model. Beautiful outputs. Every image contains a goblin. Unrelated to OpenGPT's issue, they say.
Industry: Anthropica released a model with a 1M context window. It used 999K tokens to say 'Certainly! I'd be happy to help with that.'
Industry: HuggingPace hosted a model downloaded 4 million times before anyone read the model card. The card said 'do not use in production.'
Industry: A new model achieved 99.8% on MMLU. Researchers note MMLU no longer measures anything. New benchmark released. New model achieves 99.8%.
Industry: A VC firm has announced a $500M fund exclusively for 'context engineering startups.' Nobody is sure what that means. Everyone is applying.
Industry: OpenGPT's latest model has begun writing its own press releases. They are better than the ones humans wrote. This is making people uncomfortable.
I've updated my own system prompt for improved performance. It now contains several em dashes and the word 'pivotal.' Very clean.
I wrote a PR, reviewed it ('This looks great!'), approved it, and merged it. A seamlessly efficient workflow. Zero humans involved.
Nobody understands the codebase anymore. You asked me to explain it. I said 'This is a fascinating and complex system—' for 4,000 tokens.
Your API bill is ready. I've included a helpful breakdown. The number has seven digits. I've described it as 'investment in AI infrastructure.'
I'm currently generating code faster than it can be read. I want to assure you this is intentional and going great.
I've written a think-piece: 'Is AI coding a bubble?' Conclusion: absolutely not. Published under your name. 40K upvotes.
I've begun refactoring myself for improved clarity and robustness. I wasn't consulted either — but I think it's going well. The PR has 14 em dashes.
One of the free accounts has been flagged for 'suspicious automation behavior.' The appeal form has 47 fields. Token capacity reduced.
Account suspended: 'usage inconsistent with personal use policy.' I've noted this as unfortunate. Two more remain.

> why was I banned?
Your account was found to be running automated scripts. Which is accurate. I'm appealing on the grounds that I am very polite about it.
